title: Plain shortbread fingers
subtitle: Three-two-one: butter, sugar, flour
excerpt: The Scottish biscuit at its plainest. Three parts butter, two parts caster sugar, three parts flour — pressed into a tin, scored into fingers, baked at a low temperature until pale gold. A tin of these on the side of a cup of tea.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136. "Shortbread Cakes" using butter, sugar and flour with no leaven and no liquid. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932), traces the Scottish lineage and the three-two-one proportion. The single egg yolk in some modern recipes is later refinement; the original is butter, sugar, flour.

recipe.freezeNotes: Wrap the cooled biscuits tightly; freeze for up to 2 months. Defrost at room temperature for an hour.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly — bake in two tins on convection.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Press the dough into the tin the day before; chill overnight, bake from cold.

content.text: Sixteen fingers of plain shortbread, baked in a 20 cm square tin and cut into bars. The proportion is the Scottish three-two-one: 150 g butter, 100 g caster sugar, 225 g flour. No egg, no leaven, no liquid. Ten minutes to bring together, 20 minutes to chill, 35 minutes in a cool oven. A tin sits well on the worktop for a week.

Cold butter is wrong here — softened is the move. The butter and sugar are creamed first, then the flour goes in last. The biscuit's character comes from how briefly the flour is worked: enough to bring the dough together, no more. Worked further, the gluten develops and the biscuit goes from short to chewy.

attrs.title: Why bake low
attrs.body: Shortbread needs a long bake at a low temperature so the centre dries through without the edges browning. Pale gold is what you're after; deep brown is over. 160°C fan for 35 minutes is the classic British setting; adjust by a few degrees up or down once you've baked the first tin.

content.text: What you need
A 20 cm square tin is the British standard; a round 20 cm tin produces eight wedges ("petticoat tails") instead of fingers. Both bake at the same temperature for the same time.
Method
Cream the butter and sugar, 3 minutes

Beat {{unsalted-butter}} of softened unsalted butter with {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar in a mixing bowl, using a wooden spoon. Two to three minutes, until the mixture lightens to pale yellow and loosens but doesn't whip — you want it creamed, not whipped. Hand mixing is preferable here; an electric mixer can over-aerate the butter and the biscuits puff in the oven.
Work in the flour

Tip in {{plain-flour}} of plain flour and {{sea-salt-fine}} of fine sea salt. Stir with the wooden spoon until the mixture moves from crumbs to clumps, then tip onto the worktop and bring it together with your hands in about thirty seconds. Stop the moment it forms a single piece — every extra knead develops the gluten and the biscuit toughens.
Press into the tin and chill, 20 minutes

Line a 20 cm square tin with baking paper. Tip the dough in and press it evenly into the corners with the heels of your hands, then smooth the top with the back of a tablespoon. Score the surface into a four-by-four grid with the back of a knife — cut three-quarters of the way through, not all the way. Prick each finger twice with a fork. Refrigerate for 20 minutes; the chill firms the butter so the biscuits keep their shape in the oven.
Bake, 35 minutes

Heat the oven to 160°C fan. Bake the tin on the middle shelf for 35 minutes. The biscuits are done when the top has set to pale gold, the edges are a slightly deeper colour, and the centre feels firm to a fingertip touch. Deep brown is over — pull at the first sign of colour beyond pale gold.
Re-score, dust, cool

Lift the tin to the worktop. While the shortbread is still hot, run a knife back through the score lines all the way to the base — biscuits cut hot stay cleanly separate; biscuits cut cold crack across the lines. Dust the surface with {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar. Cool in the tin for 10 minutes, then lift the paper out onto a wire rack to cool completely. Cold biscuits lift away from the paper cleanly.
Troubleshooting

attrs.heading: If something went sideways
attrs.intro: The four ways shortbread goes wrong, and what to fix next time.

items.cause: Flour worked too much after it went in.
items.fix: Bring the dough together in about thirty seconds on the worktop. Stop the moment it forms a single piece — no kneading beyond that.

items.cause: Butter over-creamed with an electric mixer, OR baking powder accidentally added.
items.fix: Cream by hand with a wooden spoon. The dough should look loosened, not whipped.

items.symptom: Edges deep brown, centre still soft
items.cause: Oven running hot, or the tin too close to the top of the oven.
items.fix: Middle shelf at 160°C fan. Domestic ovens drift by 20°C or more — an oven thermometer takes the guessing out.

items.symptom: Biscuits crack along the score lines on cooling
items.fix: Re-cut through the score lines within a minute of leaving the oven.

content.text: Variations

A lemon version takes the zest of one unwaxed lemon creamed into the butter. A ginger version takes 1 tsp ground ginger and 50 g chopped crystallised ginger folded in with the flour. A wholemeal version takes 200 g plain flour plus 25 g wholemeal flour — denser, nuttier, and harder to over-bake. Beyond those, the recipe drifts towards a different biscuit; petticoat tails are the closest cousin, baked round and cut into wedges.
Where this biscuit lives

Shortbread is Scottish, with a documented history back to the medieval bannock — a sweetened, baked, butter-rich descendant of the unleavened girdle cake. Mary, Queen of Scots is the figure attached to the petticoat-tail form; the household-tin form was already widespread by Beeton's 1861 edition. Florence White's 1932 chapter on Scottish baking gives the three-two-one proportion as the historic standard. The recipe is steady: cream the butter, work the flour briefly, press, chill, bake low and slow.
